structured_choices.data_source: Histopathological images and clinical metadata from multi-center clinical trials, 10,000+ breast cancer patients, 5-year follow-up period. All images and metadata collected at time of initial diagnosis/treatment (baseline). Recurrence events recorded prospectively during the 5-year follow-up window.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: recurrence within 5 years post-treatment (1 = recurrence event, 0 = no recurrence or censoring after 5 years). Recurrence defined as locoregional or distant metastatic disease confirmed by imaging or pathology. Censoring handled via survival analysis: patients lost to follow-up or without recurrence event by 5 years treated as censored at last known follow-up date.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified random split by site and recurrence status (80% train / 10% validation / 10% test). Stratification ensures each split maintains the recurrence rate and site distribution of the full cohort, addressing multi-center variability. Random (not temporal) split is appropriate here because all baseline data (images, clinical features) and outcomes are recorded retrospectively post-follow-up; temporal leakage is not a concern since we are not predicting forward from a training window into an unseen future window. However, if any preprocessing or outcome labeling differs by site, site-stratification prevents one site from dominating any split.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: Image normalization and augmentation parameters (e.g., Macenko stain normalization, histogram equalization settings) are fit on training set images only, then applied identically to validation and test sets. Clinical feature imputation (mean/median) and scaling (standardization) are fit on training set, applied to validation and test. No information from validation or test sets is used to set preprocessing hyperparameters.
structured_choices.features: Raw input: histopathological image patches (standardized size, e.g., 224×224 pixels) extracted from digitized slides; CNN automatically learns convolutional filters. Clinical features (age, tumor size, grade, ER/PR/HER2 status, treatment type) are embedded as a separate input stream and concatenated before final classification layers, or used in stratified sampling only. Do not use post-treatment outcomes (e.g., chemotherapy response) as features, as these are causally downstream of the baseline recurrence risk.
structured_choices.model: CNN with convolutional blocks + global average pooling + optional clinical feature fusion layer + 2–3 dense layers + sigmoid output. Architecture justified: CNNs are standard for histopathology image analysis and can capture morphological patterns predictive of recurrence. Alternative: ResNet-50 or EfficientNet backbone pretrained on natural images, fine-tuned on histopathology; transfer learning often outperforms training from scratch on medical imaging with 10k samples. Hyperparameters (learning rate, dropout, weight decay, number of filters) tuned on validation set.
structured_choices.baseline: Clinical risk score (e.g., Nottingham Prognostic Index or multi-variable Cox proportional hazards model) trained on the same clinical features (age, tumor size, grade, ER/PR/HER2, treatment) using only training set data. Baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: Cox model hyperparameters (e.g., regularization) selected via cross-validation on training set; predictions evaluated on validation set to select final threshold or parameters. Baseline does not use images. Fair comparison: CNN uses both images and clinical features; baseline uses clinical features only. This isolates the predictive value of the image modality.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Time-dependent AUC (concordance index, C-index) at 5 years. Justification: in survival analysis, C-index measures discrimination — the model's ability to rank high-risk patients higher than low-risk patients, accounting for censoring. This directly aligns with the stakeholder objective: improving follow-up strategy by accurately identifying high-recurrence-risk patients. AUC at 5 years is interpretable and actionable for clinical decision-making (e.g., intensify monitoring for top quartile).
secondary_metrics: Calibration curve (predicted recurrence probability vs. observed recurrence rate) to assess whether model confidence is well-calibrated; important for clinical adoption.
Sensitivity and specificity at clinically relevant operating points (e.g., top 20% risk = intensified monitoring threshold).
Stratified C-index by site to detect site-specific model bias or performance drift.
Stratified C-index by demographic groups (age, race if available) to check for fairness; model should perform similarly across groups.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameters and architecture choices are selected via grid search or Bayesian optimization on validation set (10% of data), with C-index on validation set as the selection criterion. The validation set is used repeatedly during tuning. Test set (10% of data) is held out and evaluated exactly once at the end of all tuning, using the selected model, to report final performance.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: Test set is never touched during training or validation. After final model selection, the test set is used exactly once to compute primary and secondary metrics. Predictions on test set are made in a single pass; no re-tuning or threshold adjustment based on test performance is permitted. If cross-validation is used for uncertainty estimation, it is applied only to training + validation pools, not involving the test set.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Stratified split by site and recurrence status ensures confounds (site-level preprocessing differences, variable recurrence prevalence) do not systematically bias any split. Baseline (clinical risk score) is evaluated in parallel and compared at the same operating point to isolate the contribution of imaging. Stratified secondary metrics (by site, demographics) detect and quantify residual confounding. In the final comparison, report both: (a) marginal improvement of CNN over baseline, and (b) improvement conditional on clinical features (i.e., does the CNN add signal beyond what clinical features alone provide?). Acknowledge limitations: this design controls for measured confounds (site, demographics, clinical features) but cannot rule out unmeasured confounds (e.g., patient adherence to follow-up, unobserved comorbidities).

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN-Based Recurrence Prediction in Breast Cancer

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses histopathological images and clinical metadata from 10,000+ breast cancer patients enrolled in multi-center clinical trials over a 5-year follow-up period. All images and clinical features (age, tumor size, grade, hormone receptor status, treatment received) are collected at baseline — at the time of initial diagnosis and treatment decision. Recurrence events (locoregional or distant metastatic disease) are recorded prospectively during follow-up and confirmed by imaging or pathology. The binary outcome is recurrence within 5 years; patients without a recurrence event or lost to follow-up are censored at their last known date.

Known data quality issues and mitigation: (a) Image quality varies across clinical sites due to differences in slide scanning, staining, and digitization protocols. This is addressed via Macenko stain normalization fit on the training set only, ensuring consistent color and contrast before CNN input. (b) Incomplete clinical metadata (e.g., missing HER2 status) is handled via mean/median imputation fit on the training set and applied consistently to validation and test. (c) Site-level imbalance (unequal numbers of patients per center) is controlled by stratified sampling (below).

### 2. Split Strategy

The dataset is divided using stratified random split: 80% training (8,000 patients), 10% validation (1,000 patients), 10% test (1,000 patients). Stratification preserves the recurrence rate and site composition within each split, ensuring no single site dominates any partition and the recurrence prevalence is balanced. Random (not temporal) stratification is appropriate because all baseline data (images, clinical features) and outcome labels are collected retrospectively post-follow-up; there is no forward-looking prediction window, so temporal leakage is not a concern. However, if image preprocessing differs by site, stratification by site prevents confounding of splits.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Raw histopathological image patches (224×224 pixels, standardized size extracted from digitized slides) are input directly to the CNN; convolutional layers automatically learn discriminative morphological features. Clinical features (age, tumor size, grade, ER/PR/HER2, treatment type) are concatenated into a separate dense layer and merged before final classification to allow the model to weight imaging and clinical signals jointly. Importantly, post-treatment outcomes (e.g., chemotherapy response, time to recurrence) are excluded as features; only information available at baseline is used.

Preprocessing steps (Macenko stain normalization, histogram equalization, clinical feature standardization) are fit on the training set only. Fitted normalization parameters and scaling statistics are then applied identically to validation and test sets, preventing data leakage from these splits into the training process.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a convolutional neural network with convolutional blocks, global average pooling, clinical feature fusion, and 2–3 dense layers with sigmoid output for binary classification. Architecture justified: CNNs are the established standard for histopathology and can capture morphological patterns associated with recurrence risk. An alternative backbone (e.g., ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet) may improve performance via transfer learning, especially given 10,000 samples is moderate for training from scratch.

The baseline is a Cox proportional hazards model trained on clinical features only (age, tumor size, grade, receptor status, treatment), without access to images. This baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: regularization hyperparameters are selected via cross-validation on the training set; predictions are evaluated on the validation set to select the optimal operating point. The baseline comparison isolates the predictive value of imaging above clinical features alone.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Time-dependent concordance index (C-index) at 5 years.** The C-index measures discrimination in the presence of censoring — the model's ability to rank high-risk patients higher than low-risk patients. This directly aligns with the stakeholder objective: improving follow-up strategies by identifying patients at high recurrence risk. C-index is interpretable (0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect ranking) and actionable for clinical deployment.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Calibration curve: predicted recurrence probability vs. observed rate, ensuring model confidence is well-calibrated for clinical use.
- Sensitivity and specificity at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., top 20% by risk score = intensified monitoring).
- Stratified C-index by site and demographic subgroups (age, race), detecting model bias or performance degradation in underrepresented populations.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (10% of data) using grid search or Bayesian optimization, with C-index on the validation set as the selection criterion. The validation set is used repeatedly during tuning. Once the final model is selected, the test set (10% of data) is held out and evaluated exactly once, in a single pass, to report final performance. No re-tuning or threshold adjustment based on test results is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Stratified split by site and recurrence prevalence ensures site-level preprocessing differences and outcome imbalance do not systematically bias any partition. The parallel evaluation of the baseline (clinical risk score) at the same operating points isolates the contribution of imaging. Stratified secondary metrics by site and demographics detect residual confounding and fairness issues.

**What the experiment establishes:** Whether a CNN trained on histopathological images achieves better discrimination (C-index) than clinical risk scores for 5-year recurrence, and whether performance is consistent across sites and demographic groups.

**What it does not establish:** Causality (imaging features do not cause recurrence), generalization beyond the training institutions, or robustness to prospective deployment under different image acquisition protocols. Unmeasured confounds (patient adherence, unobserved comorbidities) cannot be ruled out.
